act: Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act 1989
description: Law preventing atrocities and discrimination against Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes in India.

sections.title: Offences of Atrocities
sections.text: Whoever, not being a member of a Scheduled Caste or a Scheduled Tribe, intentionally insults or intimidates with intent to humiliate a member of a Scheduled Caste or Scheduled Tribe in any place within public view, shall be punishable.
sections.simplified: Insulting, humiliating, or intimidating a person from SC/ST community in public is a criminal offence. This includes caste-based slurs, social boycott, and any form of caste discrimination.
sections.punishment: Imprisonment of 6 months to 5 years, and fine

sections.title: Abuse Using Caste Name
sections.text: Whoever, not being a member of a Scheduled Caste or a Scheduled Tribe, abuses any member of a Scheduled Caste or Scheduled Tribe by caste name in any place within public view, shall be punishable.
sections.simplified: Using caste-based slurs or addressing someone by their caste name as an insult is a criminal offence under this Act.
sections.punishment: Imprisonment of 6 months to 5 years, and fine

sections.title: Denial of Entry to Public Places
sections.text: Whoever, not being a member of a Scheduled Caste or Scheduled Tribe, denies a member of a Scheduled Caste or Scheduled Tribe entry to any place of worship, educational institution, hospital, water source, or public place, shall be punishable.
sections.simplified: Denying someone from SC/ST community entry to temples, schools, hospitals, water sources, or any public place based on their caste is a crime.
sections.punishment: Imprisonment of 6 months to 5 years, and fine

sections.title: Social or Economic Boycott
sections.text: Whoever, not being a member of a Scheduled Caste or Scheduled Tribe, imposes or threatens a social or economic boycott of any member of a Scheduled Caste or Scheduled Tribe, shall be punishable.
sections.simplified: Imposing social or economic boycott on SC/ST persons (such as refusing to sell goods, not allowing participation in village functions, or threatening consequences for inter-caste marriage) is a crime.
sections.punishment: Imprisonment of 6 months to 5 years, and fine

sections.title: Special Courts
sections.text: For the purpose of providing for speedy trial, the State Government shall, with the concurrence of the Chief Justice of the High Court, by notification, establish exclusive special courts for the trial of offences under this Act.
sections.simplified: Cases under the SC/ST Act are tried in Special Courts designated specifically for speedy disposal. These courts have priority, and the government provides special public prosecutors.

sections.title: Rights of Victims and Witnesses
sections.text: The rights of victims and witnesses include: (a) right to reasonable, accurate, and timely notice of court proceedings, (b) right to be heard at any proceeding, (c) right to protection from intimidation and harassment, (d) right to travel allowance and maintenance expenses.
sections.simplified: Victims under SC/ST Act have special rights: they must be informed about all court proceedings, have a right to be heard, receive protection from threats, and get travel and maintenance expenses for attending court.

sections.title: Compensation and Relief
sections.text: The Special Court shall specify in the order for compensation the minimum amount as specified in the Schedule. The State Government shall arrange for the economic and social rehabilitation of the victim.
sections.simplified: Victims of caste-based atrocities are entitled to compensation from the government. The amount depends on the type of offence. The government must also help with rehabilitation — including housing, education, and employment assistance.
